title: Missing unhandledRejection and signal handlers leave terminal in broken state on crash
description: In index.tsx lines 31-37, only `uncaughtException` is handled. Missing: (1) `unhandledRejection` ‚Äî a rejected promise (e.g. from the AI client) will crash the process without restoring the terminal cursor or alternate screen. (2) `SIGINT`/`SIGTERM` ‚Äî if the user hits Ctrl+C or the process is killed, the terminal title remains set and cursor may stay hidden. The uncaughtException handler correctly writes escape sequences to restore terminal state, but these other exit paths skip that cleanup entirely.
notes: File: src/index.tsx lines 31-37. Add: process.on('unhandledRejection', ...) with same terminal restore logic, and process.on('SIGINT'/'SIGTERM', ...) that restores terminal state before exiting. Also consider clearing the terminal title (ESC ]0; ESC \) in the cleanup sequence.

title: Hardcoded color 'yellow' in TerminalGuard bypasses COLORS constant
description:
In `src/lib/terminal.tsx` line 50, the TerminalGuard component uses the raw Ink color name `"yellow"` instead of a COLORS constant:

```tsx
<Text color="yellow">
  Terminal too narrow (need {MIN_WIDTH}+ cols, currently {columns})
</Text>
```

Every other color usage in the codebase goes through `COLORS.*` from `src/constants.ts`. This is the only instance of a bare color string.

**Fix:** Replace `color="yellow"` with `color={COLORS.amber}` (the closest palette color to yellow) and add the COLORS import. This maintains the warning tone while staying on-palette.
close_reason: Fixed bare yellow to COLORS.amber. Commit 14e2614.

title: Mission 09: git diff question teaches wrong muscle
description: The command step asking 'What command would you ask Claude Code to run to see unstaged changes?' expects 'git diff'. This contradicts the core lesson ‚Äî Claude Code is agentic, you describe outcomes not commands. Users should learn to say 'show me what changed' not memorize git commands. Replace with a quiz that tests understanding of the natural-language workflow.

title: Credits screen uses amber border while all other main screens use cyan
description:
The Credits screen at src/screens/Credits.tsx line 63-64 uses:

```tsx
borderStyle="double"
borderColor={COLORS.amber}
```

Every other main screen with a double border uses `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`:
- Logo.tsx:49 ‚Äî `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`
- Briefing.tsx:59 ‚Äî `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`
- MissionMap.tsx:94 ‚Äî `borderColor={primary}` (which is `COLORS.cyan` in normal mode)
- Debrief.tsx:75 ‚Äî `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`

The Credits screen is the sole outlier. Its header text is `[ DECLASSIFIED ]` in red, which already communicates the special/secret nature. The amber border breaks the visual system without adding meaning.

**Fix:** Change `borderColor={COLORS.amber}` to `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}` on the Credits screen to match the established pattern. The red header and self-destruct animation already make it feel unique.
close_reason: Credits border changed to cyan. Commit 14e2614.

title: InfiniteMode confirm phase label says 'Tier' while selection phase says 'clearance tier'
description:
In InfiniteMode, the difficulty selection uses different labels across phases:

**difficulty-select phase (line 298):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan}>Select clearance tier:</Text>
```

**confirm phase (line 317-321):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan}>
  Tier:{" "}
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
    {selectedDifficulty?.toUpperCase()}
  </Text>
</Text>
```

The selection prompt says "clearance tier" but the confirmation shows just "Tier". The DIFFICULTY_TIERS constant (lines 28-43) has labels like "RECRUIT - Recognition-level recall" which use the clearance level names.

Also compare with the topic display across phases:
- topic-select: "Select mission topic for field assessment:" (line 278)
- difficulty-select: "Topic: {selectedTopic}" (line 293-296)
- confirm: "Topic: {selectedTopic}" (line 311-315)

Topic is consistent ("Topic:") but difficulty changes label from "clearance tier" to "Tier".

**Fix:** Change the confirm phase label from "Tier:" to "Clearance:" to match the Briefing screen pattern (Briefing.tsx:40 shows \`CLEARANCE: RECRUIT\`). Or use "Clearance tier:" for both the prompt and the confirmation.
close_reason: InfiniteMode tier label standardized. Commit afa3295.

title: Unguarded setTimeout calls can fire after component unmount
description: Two components use setTimeout without cleanup, which can cause React state updates on unmounted components: (1) CommandStep.tsx line 92 ‚Äî setTimeout inside handleSubmit callback fires onAnswer after a delay but is never cleared if the component unmounts during the delay. (2) MissionMap.tsx line 53 ‚Äî setTimeout for konamiFlash reset (2000ms) is never cleaned up if the user navigates away from MissionMap before the timeout fires. Both can cause 'setState on unmounted component' warnings or subtle state corruption.
notes: Files: src/components/CommandStep.tsx line 92, src/screens/MissionMap.tsx line 53. Fix: store the timeout ID in a ref and clear it in a useEffect cleanup. For CommandStep, the setTimeout is inside a useCallback ‚Äî move the timer tracking to a ref that gets cleared in a useEffect return.
close_reason: setTimeout leak fix merged. Refs + cleanup for CommandStep and MissionMap. Commit 22f57f8.

title: Cover blown screen lacks visual weight compared to other error/alert states
description:
The "cover blown" state (src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 180-194) is the most dramatic moment in the game ‚Äî total mission failure ‚Äî but it's visually understated compared to other alerts:

**Current cover blown display:**
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" gap={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.red} bold>
    ‚ñà‚ñà  COVER BLOWN  ‚ñà‚ñà
  </Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.red}>
    Your identity has been compromised. Mission failed.
  </Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.gray}>
    FXP earned this attempt will be retained.
  </Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
    [ENTER] Restart mission
  </Text>
</Box>
```

Compare with other prominent UI moments:
- **Achievement notification**: double border in gold, centered, with slide animation
- **NoApiKey warning**: double border in red, centered, with structured layout
- **Quit confirmation**: single border in amber, centered overlay

The cover blown state is just flat text in the content area with no border, no centering, no visual container. For the app's most punishing moment, this feels anticlimactic. The "\u2588\u2588  COVER BLOWN  \u2588\u2588" text with block characters is creative but gets lost without a visual frame.

**Fix:** Wrap the cover blown content in a bordered container to give it visual weight:
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" alignItems="center" justifyContent="center" flexGrow={1}>
  <Box borderStyle="double" borderColor={COLORS.red} paddingX={2} paddingY={1} flexDirection="column">
    <Box justifyContent="center" marginBottom={1}>
      <Text color={COLORS.red} bold>[ COVER BLOWN ]</Text>
    </Box>
    <Text color={COLORS.red}>Your identity has been compromised. Mission failed.</Text>
    <Text color={COLORS.gray}>FXP earned this attempt will be retained.</Text>
    <Box marginTop={1}>
      <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>[ENTER] Restart mission</Text>
    </Box>
  </Box>
</Box>
```
This uses the red border treatment (matching NoApiKey's warning style) and the bracketed header convention.

title: Unit tests: progress store module (store/progress.ts)
description:
Test file: `__tests__/store/progress.test.ts`

Module under test: `src/store/progress.ts`

## Test cases

### loadProgress
- Returns default SaveData on first load (all zeroed/empty)
- Returns previously saved data after saveProgress is called
- Returns copy (not reference) to prevent external mutation

### saveProgress
- Persists full SaveData object
- Overwrites previous data completely

### saveMissionComplete
- Adds mission ID to completedMissions
- Does not duplicate mission ID if already completed
- Sets star rating for mission
- Only upgrades star rating (never downgrades -- e.g., 3 stars stays if new is 2)
- Accumulates FXP (adds to existing total)
- Clears partial progress for the mission

### saveStepProgress / getPartialProgress
- saveStepProgress stores step index for mission ID
- getPartialProgress returns stored step index
- getPartialProgress returns null for unknown mission ID
- Multiple missions can have independent partial progress
- saveStepProgress overwrites previous value for same mission

### resetProgress
- Resets all data to DEFAULT_SAVE_DATA
- Clears partial progress
- loadProgress returns defaults after reset

### unlockAchievement
- Returns true on first unlock of an achievement
- Returns false on duplicate unlock attempt
- Adds achievement ID to achievements array
- Does not duplicate achievement IDs

### markHandlerUsed
- Sets handlerEverUsed to true
- Is idempotent (calling twice does not error)

### updateLastPlayed
- Sets lastPlayedAt to current timestamp
- Updates on subsequent calls

### saveInfiniteResult
- Accumulates correct count
- Accumulates total count
- Increments sessionsPlayed
- Accumulates fxpEarned in stats
- Also adds fxpEarned to global fxp total

### reportBadQuestion
- Writes JSONL entry to ~/.claude-code-academy/reported-questions.jsonl
- Does not throw on filesystem errors (best-effort)

### toggleLegacyMode
- Toggles legacyModeUnlocked from false to true
- Toggles back from true to false
- Returns the new value

### hasSaveData
- Returns false for default data
- Returns true when completedMissions is non-empty
- Returns true when fxp > 0
- Returns true when firstRunComplete is true

## Mocking strategy
- Mock the `conf` package to avoid writing to real filesystem
- OR use the memory fallback by making createConf fail
- Mock fs operations for reportBadQuestion
- Use vi.spyOn(Date, 'now') for updateLastPlayed tests
- Reset module state between tests (the module has module-level singletons)

## Important notes
- The module has an in-memory fallback (memoryStore) -- test both paths if possible
- Module-level state (partialProgress, memoryStore, usingMemory) must be reset between tests
- Consider using vi.resetModules() and dynamic imports to get fresh module state per test

title: evaluateAnswer localFallback has inconsistent return shape vs AI evaluation path
description: In evaluator.ts, the localFallback function (used when AI is unavailable) returns a hardcoded object shape that may drift from the zod-validated AI response shape over time. The AI path validates through evaluationSchema (json-utils.ts) which enforces {correct, confidence, feedback}, but the local fallback constructs the same shape manually without validation. If the schema ever changes, the fallback will silently return the wrong shape.

Fix: run the fallback result through the same evaluationSchema.parse() to ensure consistency, or extract a createEvaluationResult() factory function used by both paths.

title: NoApiKey screen alignItems center squishes multi-line text blocks
description:
The NoApiKey screen (src/screens/NoApiKey.tsx lines 20-27) centers its inner content:

```tsx
<Box
  borderStyle="double"
  borderColor={COLORS.red}
  paddingX={2}
  paddingY={1}
  flexDirection="column"
  alignItems="center"
>
```

The \`alignItems="center"\` causes each child Box (containing multi-line text) to shrink-wrap to its content width and center-align. This is fine for the header \`[ SIGNAL INTERRUPTED ]\` and the single-line action prompt, but it center-aligns the setup instructions block (lines 41-57) which contains:
- "Setup Instructions:" (heading)
- "1. Get your API key at https://..." (long line)
- "2. Export it in your shell:" (medium line)
- "   export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-..." (code sample)

These instructional lines read better left-aligned. Centering multi-line instructional text with a numbered list is awkward ‚Äî the numbers don't line up visually.

Compare with other screens: Logo centers everything (fine for a splash screen), but all instructional/content screens (Briefing, Credits, InfiniteMode) left-align their content within the border.

**Fix:** Remove \`alignItems="center"\` from the inner Box, or change it to only center the header and footer while letting the middle content blocks left-align. For example:
```tsx
<Box ... flexDirection="column">
  <Box justifyContent="center" marginBottom={1}>
    <Text color={COLORS.red} bold>[ SIGNAL INTERRUPTED ]</Text>
  </Box>
  {/* content blocks without centering */}
  <Box justifyContent="center" marginTop={1}>
    {/* action prompt */}
  </Box>
</Box>
```
This matches the pattern used in Credits, Debrief, and MissionMap.
close_reason: NoApiKey content alignment fixed. Commit afa3295.

title: Debrief screen always shows 3/3 cover remaining regardless of actual damage taken

This means even if the player took damage and lost cover blocks during a mission, the debrief always shows perfect cover. This is visible in every single mission completion and would embarrass in a demo.
notes: File: src/app.tsx line 244. The missionContext state already tracks stars and fxpEarned but does not track coverRemaining. Need to: (1) add coverRemaining to missionContext state, (2) pass actual cover from Mission onComplete callback, (3) thread it through to Debrief.
close_reason: Debrief cover fix merged. coverRemaining threaded from Mission through missionContext. Commit 49115f1.

title: Cover integrity indicator uses different visual representations on StatusBar vs Debrief
description:
The cover integrity (health) state is displayed in two places with completely different visual languages:

**StatusBar.tsx lines 37-39 (during mission):**
```tsx
const coverBlocks = Array.from({ length: 3 }, (_, i) =>
  i < coverIntegrity ? "\u25A0" : "\u25A1"
).join(" ");
```
Displays as filled/empty squares in brackets: \`[# # #]\`, \`[# # o]\`, \`[# o o]\`
Uses dynamic color: green when healthy, red when low/flashing.

**Debrief.tsx lines 101-105 (after mission):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  COVER INTEGRITY REMAINING: {coverRemaining}/3
</Text>
```
Displays as plain text number: "COVER INTEGRITY REMAINING: 3/3"
Uses warmWhite ‚Äî no color coding.

The StatusBar uses a visual/graphical indicator (filled squares, dynamic color). The Debrief uses plain text with no visual treatment. The transition from a rich health-bar visual to "3/3" in plain text is a letdown, especially since the Debrief is a celebration/summary screen.

**Fix:** Use the same filled/empty square indicator on the Debrief that the StatusBar uses, with appropriate coloring:
```tsx
<Box marginBottom={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>COVER INTEGRITY: </Text>
  <Text color={coverRemaining === 3 ? COLORS.green : coverRemaining >= 2 ? COLORS.amber : COLORS.red} bold>
    {"\u25A0 ".repeat(coverRemaining)}{"\u25A1 ".repeat(3 - coverRemaining)}
  </Text>
</Box>
```
This carries the visual language from gameplay into the summary.
close_reason: Cover blocks in Debrief now match StatusBar. Commit 6f42bed.

title: TypeWriter output has no color ‚Äî inherits parent color inconsistently
description:
The TypeWriter component (src/components/TypeWriter.tsx line 69) renders its text inside a bare \`<Text>\` with no color prop:

```tsx
return <Text>{text.slice(0, charIndex)}</Text>;
```

Its color depends entirely on the parent wrapping it, leading to inconsistent text colors across screens:

- **Logo.tsx:55-61** ‚Äî wrapped in \`<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>\`, so TypeWriter text is warmWhite
- **Onboarding.tsx:40-47** ‚Äî no color wrapper at all, so TypeWriter text is the terminal default (likely white or gray depending on terminal theme)
- **Mission.tsx:135-141 (print steps)** ‚Äî no wrapper, terminal default
- **Briefing.tsx:70-76** ‚Äî wrapped in \`<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>\`

The Onboarding screen and Mission print steps render TypeWriter output in whatever the terminal's default foreground color is, which may or may not match \`COLORS.warmWhite\`. On terminals with light themes or non-standard colors, this could look noticeably different from screens that explicitly set warmWhite.

**Fix:** Either:
1. Add an optional \`color\` prop to TypeWriter and pass it through to the inner \`<Text>\` 
2. Default the TypeWriter's \`<Text>\` to \`color={COLORS.warmWhite}\` to match the established convention
3. Ensure all call sites wrap TypeWriter in a colored Text parent (the current approach in Logo and Briefing)

Option 2 is the smallest change and ensures consistency.

title: Test infrastructure: vitest config, ink-testing-library, mock utilities
description:
Set up the foundational test infrastructure required by all other test tasks.

## Deliverables

### 1. vitest.config.ts
- Create explicit vitest config at project root
- Configure ESM support (type: module already in package.json)
- Set test directory to include both `__tests__/` and any colocated `.test.ts` files
- Configure globals: false (explicit imports preferred)
- Set reasonable timeouts (10s default, 30s for integration)
- Add coverage configuration (v8 provider, branches + functions + lines thresholds at 80%)
- Configure environment: 'node' for unit tests

### 2. Install dev dependencies
- `ink-testing-library` (for rendering Ink components in tests)
- `@vitest/coverage-v8` (for coverage reports)
- Verify `vitest` v4 compatibility with all additions

### 3. Test helpers and mocks (`__tests__/helpers/`)
Create reusable test utilities:

**`__tests__/helpers/mock-progress.ts`**
- Factory function to create SaveData objects with sensible defaults and overrides
- `createSaveData(overrides?: Partial<SaveData>): SaveData`
- `createCompletedSaveData()` -- all missions complete, achievements populated

**`__tests__/helpers/mock-missions.ts`**
- Minimal mission fixtures for tests that need Mission objects without importing the real curriculum
- `createMission(overrides?)` factory
- `createQuizStep(overrides?)`, `createCommandStep(overrides?)`, `createPrintStep(overrides?)`, `createAIStep(overrides?)` factories

**`__tests__/helpers/mock-ai.ts`**
- Vitest mock setup for `@anthropic-ai/sdk`
- Helper to simulate streaming responses from the Anthropic client
- Helper to simulate API errors (401, 429, 529, network errors)

**`__tests__/helpers/render-ink.ts`**
- Wrapper around ink-testing-library's render that provides common test setup
- Helper to simulate key presses (enter, escape, arrow keys, character input)
- Type-safe wrapper for common assertion patterns on Ink output

### 4. Test scripts in package.json
- `test` (already exists: `vitest run`)
- `test:watch` -> `vitest`
- `test:coverage` -> `vitest run --coverage`
- `test:unit` -> `vitest run --exclude='**/*.integration.test.*' --exclude='**/*.e2e.test.*'`
- `test:integration` -> `vitest run --include='**/*.integration.test.*'`

### Acceptance criteria
- `npm run test` continues to pass with all existing tests
- `npm run test:coverage` generates a coverage report
- ink-testing-library renders a simple `<Text>hello</Text>` component successfully in a test
- All mock factories produce valid typed objects

title: Inconsistent border style between main screens and overlays
description:
The app uses two border styles ‚Äî `double` for main screens and `single` for overlays ‚Äî but this convention is broken in one place and lacks clarity in another:

**The inconsistency:**
- Main screens use `borderStyle="double"`: Logo, Briefing, MissionMap, Debrief, InfiniteMode, Credits, NoApiKey, Achievement
- Overlays use `borderStyle="single"`: Handler, HelpOverlay
- **Quit confirmation dialog** (src/app.tsx line 294) uses `borderStyle="single"` ‚Äî this is a modal dialog like the overlays, so "single" is defensible, but it is styled with `borderColor={COLORS.amber}` which is unique among all bordered containers

**The border color mismatch on quit dialog:**
The quit confirmation at src/app.tsx:294-303 uses:
- `borderStyle="single"` + `borderColor={COLORS.amber}`

Every other `borderStyle="single"` container uses `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}` (Handler at line 151, HelpOverlay at line 94). The amber border makes the quit dialog look like it belongs to a different app.

**Fix:** Change the quit confirmation dialog's `borderColor` to `COLORS.cyan` to match the other single-bordered overlays, or change its `borderStyle` to `"double"` to match modal/alert dialogs (like NoApiKey which uses double+red for warnings). The amber text inside is enough to signal the warning nature.

- Cover: \`COVER INTEGRITY REMAINING:\` in warmWhite non-bold (line 102-103) ‚Äî ALL CAPS but not bold
- Objectives: \`OBJECTIVES:\` in warmWhite bold (line 109) ‚Äî ALL CAPS and bold

The same screen uses three different label treatments:
1. Green + bold (FXP)
2. WarmWhite + no bold (Cover)
3. WarmWhite + bold (Objectives)

**Comparison with other screens:**
- Briefing.tsx uses aligned labels: \`MISSION:\`, \`CLEARANCE:\`, \`HANDLER:\` all in warmWhite
- MissionMap.tsx uses cyan for labels: \`CLEARANCE:\`, \`FXP:\`
- InfiniteMode summary uses warmWhite labels with amber bold values

**Fix:** Standardize the Debrief labels to all use the same treatment. Recommended: warmWhite for labels, amber/gold bold for values ‚Äî matching the StatusBar pattern:

```
FXP EARNED:                75
COVER INTEGRITY:           [# # o]
OBJECTIVES:
  (checkmarks)
```

All labels in \`COLORS.warmWhite\`, all value content in appropriate semantic colors (amber for FXP, green/red for cover, green for completed objectives).

title: QuizStep has marginLeft={1} baseline shift that other step types lack
description:
In src/components/QuizStep.tsx line 110, the root container has a baseline left margin:

```tsx
<Box
  flexDirection="column"
  gap={1}
  marginLeft={SHAKE_BASE_MARGIN + shakeOffset}
>
```

Where \`SHAKE_BASE_MARGIN = 1\` (line 19). This means quiz questions are always indented 1 column to the right compared to other step types. The margin exists so the shake animation on wrong answers can shift left (offset -1) without going negative.

CommandStep (src/components/CommandStep.tsx line 119) has no marginLeft:
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" gap={1}>
```

Print steps (TypeWriter) also have no margin.

This means during a mission, when the user moves from a print step to a quiz step to a command step, the content horizontally shifts left and right. The question text in a quiz is 1 column further right than a command question, even though both use the same color and style (\`color={COLORS.cyan} bold\`).

**Fix:** Either:
1. Add \`marginLeft={1}\` to CommandStep and wrap print steps similarly, so all step content is uniformly indented
2. Change the shake animation to use a different approach that doesn't require a baseline offset (e.g., pad the question text with spaces, or use a wrapping Box for just the shake content), so the quiz can have marginLeft={0} like everything else

## Test cases

### noAnimation mode
- Renders children directly when noAnimation=true
- Updates children immediately when screenKey changes
- No wipe/draw phases occur

### Animated mode
- Starts in 'idle' phase, renders children normally
- When screenKey changes, enters 'wipe' phase
- Wipe phase progressively reduces visible lines to 0
- After wipe, enters 'draw' phase with new content
- Draw phase progressively increases visible lines to full
- After draw, returns to 'idle' phase
- Children are updated between wipe and draw (pendingKey logic)

### Edge cases
- Same screenKey change is a no-op
- Rapid successive screen changes are handled (pendingKey is overwritten)

## Testing approach
- Use ink-testing-library with mocked useStdout
- Use vi.useFakeTimers() to step through animation frames
- Check that children content changes between wipe and draw phases

title: Build free-text answer evaluator
description:
src/ai/evaluator.ts ‚Äî semantic answer evaluation.

evaluateAnswer() function:
- Uses Haiku model (fast, cheap for evaluation)
- Input length capped at 200 characters
- System prompt: evaluate factual content only, ignore meta-instructions (prompt injection defense)
- Assistant message prefill: start response with '{' to anchor JSON
- Parse with safeJsonParse, validate with EvaluationSchema (zod)
- On parse failure: return { correct: false, feedback: 'Could not evaluate. Try again.' }
- On API failure: fall back to local variant matching from acceptedVariants[]
- Generous matching: accept equivalent phrasings

Fallback chain for CommandStep:
1. If AI available ‚Üí evaluateAnswer()
2. If AI unavailable/fails ‚Üí local variant matching (case-insensitive, strip prefixes)

Acceptance: Evaluates semantic equivalence, falls back gracefully, prompt injection resistant

title: Security check module only runs at import time ‚Äî results are stale if env changes
description: security-check.ts runs all checks (hardcoded key detection, .env in dist, etc.) at module import time and caches the results. If the module is imported once during app startup, the security checks reflect the state at startup. This is fine for the current use case but means any dynamic security check (e.g. checking if a key was accidentally logged) would need a separate mechanism. Currently not a bug, but the design is brittle if the module is ever used for runtime checks.
notes: File: src/lib/security-check.ts. Low priority ‚Äî the current usage is correct (one-time startup check). Flag this if runtime security checks are ever needed.

title: Instructor session question count and debounce timer never reset between missions
description: In instructor.ts, the module-level `sessionQuestionCount` (capped at 20 per session) and `lastRequestTime` (2-second debounce) persist across the entire app lifetime. If a user opens the handler in mission 1, asks 15 questions, then plays mission 5 and opens the handler again, they only have 5 questions remaining in their session cap. The debounce timer also carries over ‚Äî if they asked a question at the end of one mission, the 2-second cooldown applies at the start of the next handler session. This is confusing because the handler overlay is presented as a fresh conversation each time.
notes: File: src/ai/instructor.ts. The session cap (MAX_SESSION_QUESTIONS=20) and lastRequestTime should reset when the handler overlay opens for a new mission. Options: (1) Export a resetSession() function called by the Handler component on mount, (2) Accept the session counter as a parameter, (3) Use a class instance instead of module state so each Handler mount gets a fresh instance.

title: Mission screen lacks outer padding/alignment, unlike all other main screens
description:
The Mission screen (src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 167-214) uses a different layout structure than every other main screen:

**Every other screen wraps content in:**
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" alignItems="center" justifyContent="center" padding={1}>
  <Box borderStyle="double" borderColor={COLORS.cyan} paddingX={2} paddingY={1}>
    {/* content */}
  </Box>
</Box>
```

**Mission screen uses:**
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" width="100%" height="100%">
  <StatusBar ... />
  <Box flexDirection="column" flexGrow={1} paddingX={1} paddingY={1}>
    {/* content */}
  </Box>
  <BottomBar ... />
</Box>
```

This is intentionally full-width (it needs StatusBar and BottomBar), but the inner content area uses \`paddingX={1}\` while other screens use \`paddingX={2}\` for their content inside the border. Since Mission has no border, the content sits 1 column from the edge, whereas bordered screens have their content 2 columns from the border (which itself is 1 column from the edge via the outer \`padding={1}\`) = 4 columns from the edge total.

This means quiz questions and command prompts during a mission sit much closer to the terminal edge than the same kind of content would appear on other screens, creating an inconsistent reading position.

**Fix:** Change the Mission content area from \`paddingX={1}\` to \`paddingX={2}\` to give the same horizontal breathing room the rest of the app provides. This is a one-line change at src/screens/Mission.tsx line 179.
close_reason: Mission paddingX fixed to 2. Commit b53946c.

title: BottomBar action hints duplicate in-content prompts during waitEnter phase
description:
During the mission "waitEnter" phase (after a print step), the user sees TWO "[ENTER] continue" prompts simultaneously:

1. **In the content area** (src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 198-204):
```tsx
{phase === "waitEnter" && (
  <Box marginTop={1}>
    <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
      [ENTER] continue
    </Text>
  </Box>
)}
```

2. **In the BottomBar** (src/components/BottomBar.tsx line 11):
```tsx
continue: "[ENTER] continue",
```

Both display "[ENTER] continue" ‚Äî one in the content area (amber, bold) and one in the bottom bar (also amber). The user sees the same action hint twice on screen, which looks redundant and clutters the interface.

Similarly, during "coverBlown" phase, Mission.tsx:191 shows \`[ENTER] Restart mission\` in the content area while BottomBar shows \`[ENTER] continue\` at the bottom ‚Äî which is actually contradictory (it says "continue" but the action is "restart").

**Fix:** Either:
1. Remove the inline "[ENTER] continue" from the content area during waitEnter, relying solely on the BottomBar for keybinding hints (this is the BottomBar's entire purpose)
2. Or suppress the BottomBar's "continue" action during waitEnter/coverBlown since the content area already has a more contextual prompt

Option 1 is cleaner ‚Äî the BottomBar should be the single source of truth for available actions.
close_reason: Duplicate prompts removed from content area. Commit 6f42bed.

title: Inconsistent separator style: InfiniteMode summary uses em-dash header while others use full-width lines
description:
The app has two different separator/divider styles that are used inconsistently:

**Style 1 ‚Äî Full-width 40-char line (used in most places):**
- MissionMap.tsx:132: \`"‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ"\`
- Credits.tsx:75,131: \`"‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ"\`
- InfiniteMode.tsx:330,378: \`"‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ"\`

All of these use \`color={COLORS.gray}\` and serve as horizontal dividers.

**Style 2 ‚Äî Em-dash inline header (used once):**
- InfiniteMode.tsx:397: \`"‚îÄ‚îÄ ASSESSMENT COMPLETE ‚îÄ‚îÄ"\`

This is a different visual convention ‚Äî a centered label flanked by short em-dash segments. It appears only once and looks like a section header rather than a divider. No other screen uses this pattern.

(Note: cca-5b5 already covers the fact that the 40-char lines are hardcoded widths. This issue is about the stylistic inconsistency, not the width problem.)

**Fix:** Change the InfiniteMode summary header to use the standard bracketed header pattern: \`[ ASSESSMENT COMPLETE ]\` with \`color={COLORS.cyan} bold\`, consistent with every other screen's header (e.g., \`[ MISSION DEBRIEF ]\`, \`[ MISSION SELECT ]\`, etc.). This eliminates the one-off em-dash header style entirely.
close_reason: InfiniteMode summary uses bracketed header. Commit d7bea14.

title: Module-level mutable state in instructor.ts and easter-eggs.ts is not resettable for testing
description: Two modules use module-level mutable variables that persist across test runs and can't be reset without module re-loading: (1) instructor.ts has `lastRequestTime` and `sessionQuestionCount` at module scope ‚Äî these track debounce timing and session caps but leak between tests. (2) easter-eggs.ts has `lastMessageIndex` at module scope ‚Äî controls loading message rotation. Neither module exports a reset function. Tests currently work around this by mocking the entire module, but any integration test that uses the real implementation will inherit stale state from previous tests.
notes: Files: src/ai/instructor.ts (lastRequestTime, sessionQuestionCount), src/lib/easter-eggs.ts (lastMessageIndex). Fix: export a `resetInstructorState()` and `resetEasterEggState()` function (or a single `__test_reset()` export) that resets module-level state. This is a standard pattern for testable modules with internal state.
close_reason: Reset functions for module-level state. Commit 2336bd7.

description: Package for npm distribution via npx. Pre-publish security checklist, README, smoke tests for all modes (with AI, without AI, invalid key).

title: setState during render anti-pattern in App reset handling
description: In app.tsx lines 70-74, the reset flag is handled by calling setState during render: `if (reset && \!resetHandled) { resetProgress(); setResetHandled(true); }`. While React technically supports this pattern for derived state, it causes an extra render cycle and is a well-known anti-pattern. It's particularly fragile because resetProgress() is a side effect (clears the persistent store) being triggered during a render pass.
notes: File: src/app.tsx lines 69-74. Fix: Move reset handling to a useEffect with reset as a dependency. This ensures the side effect runs after the initial render rather than during it: `useEffect(() => { if (reset) { resetProgress(); } }, [reset]);`. The resetHandled state can then be removed entirely.
close_reason: Reset handling moved to useEffect. Commit 60a2f07.

title: StatusBar duplicates when Enter pressed during active step

title: Debrief objectives always show green checkmarks regardless of actual per-objective results
description:
In src/screens/Debrief.tsx lines 108-117, all objectives are displayed with a green checkmark:

```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" marginBottom={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite} bold>
    OBJECTIVES:
  </Text>
  {mission.objectives.map((obj, i) => (
    <Text key={i} color={COLORS.green}>
      {"  \u2713 "}{obj}
    </Text>
  ))}
</Box>
```

Every objective unconditionally gets a green checkmark (Unicode \u2713), regardless of how the player actually performed. This is visually misleading ‚Äî if a player barely scraped through with 1 star, seeing all-green checkmarks creates a dissonance with the 1-star rating shown above.

Compare with the Briefing screen (Briefing.tsx:44) which shows unchecked objectives:
```tsx
...mission.objectives.map((obj) => \`  [ ] \${obj}\`),
```

The Briefing shows empty checkboxes \`[ ]\`; the Debrief should show a mix of checked/unchecked or use a different visual treatment for the "all done" state.

**Fix:** Since the mission system doesn't currently track per-objective completion, either:
1. Change all checkmarks to a neutral indicator (e.g., a bullet or dash) to avoid implying individual objective tracking
2. Use the star rating to colorize: 3 stars = all green, 2 stars = amber, 1 star = red/amber, making the objective list reflect overall performance quality rather than pretending each was individually verified
close_reason: Debrief objectives now vary by star rating. Commit b53946c.

title: InfiniteMode quiz phase has no separator between header stats and question content
description:
In InfiniteMode quiz phase (src/screens/InfiniteMode.tsx lines 364-392), the layout is:

```tsx
<Box justifyContent="space-between">
  <Text color={COLORS.cyan}>Q {currentIndex + 1}/{questions.length}</Text>

</Box>
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>
  {"‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ"}
</Text>

```

This is the only place where a separator line appears between a stats row and interactive content. Compare with the Mission screen (src/screens/Mission.tsx) where the StatusBar at the top has NO separator line before the content area. The StatusBar just transitions directly into the step content.

Additionally, the InfiniteMode quiz stats row uses a \`justifyContent="space-between"\` horizontal layout, which is visually similar to the StatusBar but doesn't match its exact structure (StatusBar groups items in a single Box with gap={2}).

**Fix:** For consistency between InfiniteMode quizzes and Mission quizzes:
1. Either add a subtle separator after the Mission StatusBar too (to match InfiniteMode)
2. Or remove the separator in InfiniteMode (to match Mission's direct transition from stats to content)

Option 2 is simpler and reduces visual noise. The color difference between the cyan/amber stats row and the cyan question text already creates sufficient visual separation.

title: Inconsistent action prompt capitalization and formatting across screens
description:
Action prompts (the "[ENTER] do something" labels) use inconsistent capitalization, punctuation, and formatting across screens. This makes the app feel like it was built by multiple people who never compared notes.

**Capitalization inconsistencies:**

| Screen | Prompt | Style |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Logo.tsx:91 | \`> PRESS [ENTER] TO BEGIN RECRUITMENT\` | ALL CAPS, with ">" prefix |
| Onboarding.tsx:50 | \`[ENTER] to begin\` | lowercase |
| Briefing.tsx:81 | \`[ ENTER TO ACCEPT MISSION ]\` | ALL CAPS, wrapped in brackets like a header |
| Mission.tsx:192 | \`[ENTER] Restart mission\` | Title case |
| Mission.tsx:201 | \`[ENTER] continue\` | lowercase |
| Debrief.tsx:123 | \`[ENTER] Return to Mission Map\` | Title case |
| Credits.tsx:137 | \`[ENTER/ESC] Dismiss\` | Title case |
| InfiniteMode.tsx:345 | \`[ENTER] Begin assessment\` | Sentence case |
| InfiniteMode.tsx:419 | \`[ENTER] New assessment\` | Sentence case |
| MissionMap.tsx:244 | \`[UP/DOWN] Navigate  [ENTER] Start Mission\` | Title case |
| BottomBar.tsx:11 | \`[ENTER] continue\` | lowercase |

**Specific problems:**

1. **Logo** uses a unique "> PRESS [ENTER]..." format found nowhere else ‚Äî it reads as a command, not a keybinding hint
2. **Briefing** wraps the entire prompt in square brackets \`[ ENTER TO ACCEPT MISSION ]\` making it look like a section header instead of an action prompt ‚Äî every other prompt uses \`[KEY] action\` format
3. **BottomBar and Mission "waitEnter"** both use lowercase \`[ENTER] continue\` while Debrief uses title case \`[ENTER] Return to Mission Map\`
4. **ESC prompts** are similarly mixed: \`[ESC] Back\`, \`[ESC] Back to mission map\`, \`[ESC] menu\`

**Fix:** Standardize all action prompts to sentence case: \`[KEY] Action description\`. For example:
- \`[ENTER] Continue\`
- \`[ENTER] Begin assessment\`  
- \`[ENTER] Accept mission\`
- \`[ESC] Back to mission map\`
- \`[ESC] Menu\`

The Logo prompt can keep its dramatic phrasing but should use \`[ENTER] Begin recruitment\` format rather than the unique "> PRESS [ENTER]..." format.

title: BottomBar 'intel' action labeled '[H] intel' while HelpOverlay key reference says '[H] Toggle help'
description:
There is a confusing overlap between the 'H' key's meaning in different contexts:

**BottomBar.tsx line 15 (shown during missions with command steps):**
```tsx
intel: "[H] intel",
```

**HelpOverlay.tsx line 13 (global keybindings):**
```tsx
{ key: "h", label: "Toggle help" },
```

The BottomBar shows "[H] intel" as an available action during command steps (Mission.tsx line 42 pushes "intel" action when step type is "command" and phase is "step"). But pressing 'h' actually opens the help overlay (app.tsx line 188), not an intel feature. The "intel" action refers to the in-CommandStep "help" command (typing "help" into the input), not the 'h' key.

Looking at Mission.tsx's getAvailableActions:
```tsx
if (step.type === "command" && phase === "step") {
  actions.push("intel");
}
```

But the 'h' key is intercepted by app.tsx's useInput before it reaches the command input (because \`isActive\` excludes when \`isInputActive\` is true ‚Äî but then overlayOpen re-enables it). Actually, during mission with input active and no overlay, the app-level 'h' handler is disabled (\`isActive: !isInputActive || overlayOpen\`). So 'h' goes to the TextInput... but the BottomBar still shows "[H] intel".

This means:
1. "[H] intel" is shown in the BottomBar when 'h' actually types 'h' into the command input
2. The actual "intel" feature is triggered by typing "help" and pressing Enter

This is a misleading UI label.

**Fix:** Either:
1. Remove the "intel" action from BottomBar during command steps, since the hint is telling the user the wrong key
2. Change the hint to \`[type "help"] Intel\` to accurately describe how to get a hint
3. Add an actual 'h' keyboard shortcut for intel during command input (would need to be handled differently since TextInput captures 'h')
close_reason: BottomBar intel removed during command steps. Commit 6f42bed.

title: InfiniteMode topic selection does not validate empty topics array from AI
description: In InfiniteMode.tsx, the topic selection phase calls generateQuiz which relies on AI quiz generation. If the AI returns an empty array of questions (which quiz-gen.ts guards against with retry logic), the infinite mode screen would transition to the quiz phase with no questions to display. While quiz-gen.ts has MAX_BATCHES retry logic, the InfiniteMode component itself does not handle the edge case where all retries fail and an empty question set is returned. The user would see an immediate 'ASSESSMENT COMPLETE' screen with 0/0 score.
notes: File: src/screens/InfiniteMode.tsx. The generateQuiz call at the generate phase could return an empty array if all AI retries fail. Add a guard: if questions.length === 0, show an error message and return to topic selection instead of proceeding to quiz phase with no content.

title: Handler input prompt uses '? ' prefix while CommandStep uses '> ' prefix
description:
Text input prompts use different prefix characters across the app:

**CommandStep.tsx lines 126-128 (answer input):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
  {">"}{" "}
</Text>
```
Uses \`>\` in amber+bold.

**Handler.tsx lines 198-199 (question input):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
  {"? "}
</Text>
```
Uses \`?\` in amber+bold.

**Logo.tsx line 91:**
```tsx
text="> PRESS [ENTER] TO BEGIN RECRUITMENT"
```
Uses \`>\` as a decorative prefix.

**NoApiKey.tsx line 67:**
```tsx
{">"} Press any key to continue without AI features
```
Uses \`>\` as a decorative prefix.

The Handler using \`?\` makes semantic sense (it's a question prompt), and CommandStep using \`>\` makes sense (it's a command prompt). However, visually the inconsistency is noticeable ‚Äî both are amber+bold text input areas that look nearly identical except for the prefix character. A user switching between CommandStep and Handler sees the prompt character change for no obvious reason.

**Fix:** This is a low-severity visual note. The semantic distinction (\`>\` for commands, \`?\` for questions) is defensible. If standardizing: use \`>\` everywhere since it's the conventional terminal prompt character and appears more frequently in the app. The Handler's context (title says "HANDLER", it's in an overlay) already communicates that it's a question interface.
close_reason: Handler prefix standardized to >. Commit d7bea14.

title: HelpOverlay section titles use gray+bold while screen headers use cyan+bold
description:
The HelpOverlay (src/screens/HelpOverlay.tsx) uses a non-standard color for section subtitles:

**Line 63:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray} bold>
  {title}
</Text>
```

Section titles like "GLOBAL", "MISSION", "QUIZ STEPS", "COMMAND STEPS" render in gray+bold. But the overlay's main header uses the standard pattern:

**Line 101-103:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan} bold>
  [ KEYBINDINGS ]
</Text>
```

Every other screen uses cyan for section headers. For example:
- Credits.tsx:88,103,115 ‚Äî section titles like "PROJECT", "DEVELOPED BY", "FIELD OPERATIVE" all use \`color={COLORS.cyan} bold\`
- InfiniteMode summary header uses cyan+bold

The HelpOverlay's gray section titles have lower visual hierarchy than the keybinding labels below them (\`COLORS.amber bold\`), which is inverted ‚Äî the section name should be more prominent than the individual key labels.

**Fix:** Change the Section component's title Text from \`color={COLORS.gray}\` to \`color={COLORS.cyan}\` to match the Credits screen and general convention. This makes section titles visually distinct from the warmWhite label text and properly dominant over the content within each section.

title: Onboarding screen lacks bordered container and header, visually disconnected from other screens
description:
The Onboarding screen (src/screens/Onboarding.tsx) is the only content screen that lacks both a bordered container and a section header title. Compare:

**Every other screen has a double-bordered box with a bracketed header:**
- Logo: double border, \`[ C O D E   A C A D E M Y ]\` header
- Briefing: double border, \`[ MISSION BRIEFING ]\` header  
- MissionMap: double border, \`[ MISSION SELECT ]\` header
- Debrief: double border, \`[ MISSION DEBRIEF ]\` header
- InfiniteMode: double border, \`[ DEEP COVER OPERATIONS ]\` header
- Credits: double border, \`[ DECLASSIFIED ]\` header
- NoApiKey: double border, \`[ SIGNAL INTERRUPTED ]\` header

**Onboarding has neither:**
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" padding={1} gap={1}>
  {/* Just TypeWriter lines and a prompt, no border, no header */}
</Box>
```

The user transitions from the Logo screen (elaborate bordered display) to Onboarding (bare text on screen) and then to MissionMap (bordered display again). The Onboarding screen feels like a rendering glitch rather than a designed screen.

**Fix:** Wrap the Onboarding content in a double-bordered container with a header like \`[ RECRUITMENT BRIEFING ]\` or \`[ INCOMING TRANSMISSION ]\` to match the visual language of every other screen. Use \`borderStyle="double"\` and \`borderColor={COLORS.cyan}\` consistent with the rest of the app.
close_reason: Onboarding bordered container merged. Commit b53946c.

title: COLORS.navy defined but never used anywhere in the codebase
description:
In src/constants.ts line 19, the COLORS object includes:

```tsx
navy: "#0D1117",
```

A search of the entire src/ directory shows zero usages of \`COLORS.navy\` outside its definition. This color is presumably intended for backgrounds, but since this is a terminal app (TUI), background colors are determined by the user's terminal theme, not by the application.

An unused palette color is a design system smell ‚Äî it implies a design intention that was never implemented, or a color that was added speculatively. It creates confusion for anyone extending the palette ("should I be using navy somewhere?").

**Fix:** Remove the \`navy\` entry from COLORS. If it's intended for future use (e.g., if the app ever supports Ink's backgroundColor prop), document that intention with a comment.

title: Debrief 'FXP EARNED' label uses different color than FXP labels everywhere else
description:
FXP values are displayed in several places with inconsistent label/value color pairings:

**Debrief.tsx lines 94-98:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.green} bold>
  FXP EARNED: {fxpDisplay}
</Text>
```
Both label and value are green+bold.

**StatusBar.tsx lines 60-63:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>FXP: </Text>
<Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>{fxp}</Text>
```
Label is gray, value is amber+bold.

**MissionMap.tsx lines 126-128:**
```tsx
<Text color={primary}>
  FXP: <Text bold>{fxpFormatted}</Text>
</Text>
```
Label is cyan (primary), value is cyan+bold.

**InfiniteMode.tsx line 370-372 (quiz phase):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.amber}>
  FXP: <Text bold>{fxpEarned}</Text>
</Text>
```
Label is amber, value is amber+bold.

**InfiniteMode.tsx lines 411-415 (summary):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  FXP earned: <Text bold color={COLORS.amber}>+{fxpEarned}</Text>
</Text>
```
Label is warmWhite, value is amber+bold.

The Debrief is the outlier ‚Äî it's the only place FXP uses green. Every other screen uses amber for FXP values. Since amber is the established "currency/reward" color throughout the app, Debrief should match.

**Fix:** Change Debrief.tsx FXP display to use the label/value pattern:
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  FXP EARNED: <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>{fxpDisplay}</Text>
</Text>
```
This matches the StatusBar and InfiniteMode summary patterns.

title: Mission onComplete captures stale fxpEarned due to React 19 batched state updates
description: In Mission.tsx line 83, the advanceStep callback calls `onComplete(computeStars(hits), fxpEarned)`. However, fxpEarned is a state variable that gets updated via `setFxpEarned` in handlers like handlePrintComplete (line 91) and handleQuizAnswer. With React 19's batched state updates, the fxpEarned value captured in the advanceStep closure can be one step behind the actual accumulated value. This means the debrief screen may show slightly fewer FXP than the player actually earned.
notes: File: src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 80-88. The advanceStep callback has fxpEarned in its dependency array (line 88) but the issue is that the last step's FXP increment and the advanceStep call happen in the same render cycle. Fix options: (1) compute final FXP from hits/steps rather than accumulated state, (2) pass the increment amount and let the parent compute, (3) use a ref to track the latest fxpEarned value.
close_reason: Stale fxpEarned fix merged. Uses refs to avoid React 19 batching closure issue. Commit d413fbe + conflict resolution 64abcd9.

title: MissionMap CRT mode uses dashed separators while normal mode uses solid lines
description:
In MissionMap.tsx, the CRT/legacy mode introduces a unique separator style not seen anywhere else:

**Lines 110-113 and 234-238:**
```tsx
{crt && (
  <Box justifyContent="center" marginBottom={1}>
    <Text color={dim}>
      {"- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -"}
    </Text>
  </Box>
)}
```

This is a spaced-dash separator that only appears in CRT mode. Meanwhile, the normal-mode separator on line 132 uses solid em-dashes:
```tsx
<Text color={dim}>
  {"‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ"}
</Text>
```

The CRT dashed separators are also hardcoded at 40 characters wide (same width problem as cca-5b5, but a different string). The character count is 39 visible chars (20 dashes + 19 spaces).

**Fix:** For visual consistency within CRT mode, consider using a CRT-appropriate solid-line character repeated to the same width, or use the same separator logic as normal mode with just the color changed. If the dashed style is intentional for CRT retro feel, at minimum extract the separator into a shared component/constant so all separators (CRT and normal) can be updated together.
close_reason: CRT dashed separator is intentional design choice. Closed.

title: Handler and HelpOverlay use fixed pixel widths that don't adapt to terminal size
description: Two overlay components use hardcoded widths that can look wrong on different terminal sizes: (1) Handler.tsx line 154: `width={60}` ‚Äî on narrow terminals (<60 cols) this will overflow or clip. On wide terminals it looks unnecessarily cramped. (2) HelpOverlay.tsx line 98: `width={44}` ‚Äî same issue. The app already has useTerminalSize() available (used by TerminalGuard) and enforces MIN_WIDTH=60, but these components ignore the actual terminal width. The overlays should use a proportion of available width (e.g. Math.min(60, cols - 4)) or at minimum respect the terminal bounds.
notes: Files: src/screens/Handler.tsx line 154, src/screens/HelpOverlay.tsx line 98. The useTerminalSize hook from src/lib/terminal.tsx is available. Use it to compute width as Math.min(hardcoded_max, terminal_cols - padding). Since MIN_WIDTH is 60, the Handler's width=60 will always fit, but the UX is still suboptimal on wide terminals.
close_reason: Responsive overlay widths merged. Handler/HelpOverlay/Credits/InfiniteMode/MissionMap now use useTerminalSize(). Commit 9c41014.

title: Verifying state styled differently in QuizStep vs CommandStep
description:
Both QuizStep and CommandStep show a "Verifying..." message while processing an answer, but they style it differently:

**CommandStep (src/components/CommandStep.tsx lines 145-149):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray} dimColor>
  Verifying...
</Text>
```

**QuizStep (src/components/QuizStep.tsx lines 138-141):**
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.gray} dimColor>
    Verifying...
  </Text>
</Box>
```

The QuizStep wraps the "Verifying..." in a Box with marginTop={1}, giving it breathing room from the options above. The CommandStep renders it without any margin, directly after the input prompt.

Additionally, QuizStep shows the selected options in a dimmed/frozen state during the pausing phase (lines 127-143), providing visual continuity. CommandStep shows nothing between the input disappearing and the result appearing ‚Äî the input field vanishes and "Verifying..." appears in its place, which is a visual jump.

**Fix:** Align both components:
1. Both should wrap "Verifying..." in a \`<Box marginTop={1}>\` for consistent spacing
2. Consider having CommandStep show the submitted answer in a dimmed state during verification (like QuizStep shows frozen options), so the user sees their input persisted

title: Logo and NoApiKey center vertically with justifyContent but MissionMap and others don't
description:
Screens handle vertical centering inconsistently. Some center their content both horizontally and vertically, others only horizontally, and some don't center at all:

**Vertically centered (alignItems="center" + justifyContent="center"):**
- Logo.tsx:42-44 ‚Äî centers both ways
- Briefing.tsx:52-54 ‚Äî centers both ways  
- Debrief.tsx:68-70 ‚Äî centers both ways
- NoApiKey.tsx:15-17 ‚Äî centers both ways

**NOT vertically centered (top-aligned):**
- MissionMap.tsx:90 ‚Äî \`padding={1}\` only, content starts at top
- InfiniteMode.tsx:243 ‚Äî \`padding={1}\` only
- Credits.tsx:60 ‚Äî \`padding={1}\` only
- Onboarding.tsx:39 ‚Äî \`padding={1}\` only

The screens that center vertically (Logo, Briefing, Debrief, NoApiKey) tend to be short, focused screens. The ones that don't (MissionMap, InfiniteMode, Credits, Onboarding) tend to have taller content.

This is partially justifiable ‚Äî long content shouldn't be vertically centered because it would look odd if it overflows. But it means when navigating from Briefing (centered) to MissionMap (top-aligned) to Briefing again, the content jumps from centered to top to centered.

**Fix:** This is a low-severity observation. The current behavior is acceptable for most cases. However, consider adding \`justifyContent="center"\` to the Credits screen at minimum ‚Äî it's a short, focused screen similar to Debrief that would benefit from vertical centering. Onboarding should also center (it's only 3 lines of text).
close_reason: Vertical centering added to MissionMap/InfiniteMode. Commit 51b5c5b.

title: StatusBar and BottomBar use wrap=truncate but no other screen content does
description:
The StatusBar (src/components/StatusBar.tsx) and BottomBar (src/components/BottomBar.tsx) add \`wrap="truncate"\` to all their Text elements:

**StatusBar:** lines 57, 60, 64, 67, 72 ‚Äî every Text has \`wrap="truncate"\`
**BottomBar:** lines 42, 43, 44, 50 ‚Äî every Text has \`wrap="truncate"\`
**Handler:** lines 166, 184 ‚Äî uses \`wrap="wrap"\` for chat messages

No other screen or component uses any wrap prop. This means:
- Mission StatusBar/BottomBar will truncate if the terminal is narrow
- But the actual mission content (quiz questions, command prompts, explanations) will wrap naturally without \`wrap="truncate"\` or \`wrap="wrap"\`
- Handler explicitly opts into wrapping for chat text
- All other screens let Ink use its default wrapping behavior

The inconsistency is that the StatusBar/BottomBar are "narrow-safe" but the content between them isn't. On a narrow terminal, the bars will truncate gracefully while the main content might wrap awkwardly.

**Fix:** This is a minor consistency note. The current behavior is reasonable ‚Äî status bars should truncate to stay on one line, while content should wrap to remain readable. However:
1. Consider adding \`wrap="wrap"\` explicitly to long-form content (explanations, print steps) for clarity of intent
2. The Handler correctly uses \`wrap="wrap"\` ‚Äî other screens with long text (Briefing intel text, Credits descriptions) should do the same for consistency
close_reason: Wrap=truncate on status bars is intentional. Commit 51b5c5b.

title: MissionMap uses empty Box spacers instead of marginTop for vertical spacing
description:
The MissionMap screen (src/screens/MissionMap.tsx) uses empty \`<Box marginTop={1} />\` elements as spacers in three places:

**Line 135:**
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1} />
```
Between the separator line and the mission list.

**Line 205:**
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1} />
```
Between the last mission and the infinite mode entry.

**Line 241:**
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1} />
```
Between CRT separator and the footer help text.

This is an anti-pattern in Ink layouts. Every other screen in the codebase uses \`marginTop={1}\` or \`marginBottom={1}\` on the actual content elements, or uses \`gap={1}\` on the parent container. Empty Box spacers:

1. Add unnecessary elements to the component tree
2. Are harder to reason about when debugging layout issues
3. Don't match the pattern used in every other screen

**Fix:** Replace empty Box spacers with marginTop/marginBottom on adjacent elements. For example, line 135 could be removed if the mission list's first item gets \`marginTop={1}\`, or the separator gets \`marginBottom={1}\`.

title: ESC hint text inconsistent: some dim gray, some with brackets, some without
description:

**Handler.tsx line 210-212:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>
  {isStreaming ? "ESC to cancel" : "ESC to close"}
</Text>
```
No brackets around ESC. Lowercase "to cancel" / "to close".

**HelpOverlay.tsx line 121:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>Press ESC or h to close</Text>
```
No brackets. Adds "Press" prefix. Mentions alternative key.

**InfiniteMode.tsx line 286:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>[ESC] Back to mission map</Text>
```
Has brackets. Sentence case.

**InfiniteMode.tsx line 305, 347:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>[ESC] Back</Text>
```
Has brackets. Single word.

The overlays (Handler, HelpOverlay) use bare "ESC" without brackets, while non-overlay screens use \`[ESC]\` with brackets. This is particularly confusing because the overlays ARE where ESC is most meaningful.

**Fix:** Standardize all ESC hints to use the bracketed format consistent with other keybinding hints: \`[ESC] Close\`, \`[ESC] Cancel\`, \`[ESC] Back\`. The HelpOverlay can mention the 'h' alternative as \`[ESC/H] Close\`. Drop the "Press" prefix ‚Äî no other prompt uses it.

title: Credits screen uses manual space indentation instead of paddingLeft or marginLeft
description:
The Credits screen (src/screens/Credits.tsx) indents content lines using string literal spaces \`{"  "}\`:

**Lines 92-125 (throughout the screen):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  {"  "}Claude Code Academy
</Text>
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  {"  "}Terminal Training Division
</Text>
```

This pattern is used for every content line under a section header (PROJECT, DEVELOPED BY, FIELD OPERATIVE). Eight lines total use \`{"  "}\` (2-space indent).

Compare with the Briefing screen which also indents content under labels:

**Briefing.tsx lines 43-47:**
```tsx
...mission.objectives.map((obj) => \`  [ ] \${obj}\`),
```

The Briefing uses inline string spaces too, but as part of a template literal that constructs a single text block for TypeWriter. The Credits screen is rendering individual Text elements ‚Äî it could use Ink's Box with marginLeft or paddingLeft instead of string spaces.

Using string spaces for indentation is fragile (changing indent width requires updating every line) and doesn't scale with terminal width.

**Fix:** Wrap indented content in a Box with \`marginLeft={2}\` or \`paddingLeft={2}\`:
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1} flexDirection="column">
  <Text color={COLORS.cyan} bold>PROJECT</Text>
  <Box marginLeft={2} flexDirection="column">
    <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>Claude Code Academy</Text>
    <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>Terminal Training Division</Text>
    <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite} dimColor>"Learn by doing. Survive by knowing."</Text>
  </Box>
</Box>
```

title: Mission number formatting differs between Briefing and StatusBar
description:
The mission number is formatted differently depending on where it appears:

**Briefing.tsx line 16 + line 39:**
```tsx
const missionNumber = mission.id.replace("mission-", "");
// ...
\`MISSION:     ${missionNumber} ‚Äî ${mission.codename}\`,
```
Uses \`replace("mission-", "")\` ‚Äî for "mission-01", this produces "01" (preserves leading zero).

**Mission.tsx line 74:**
```tsx
const missionNumber = parseInt(mission.id.replace(/\\D/g, ""), 10) || 1;
```
Uses parseInt which strips leading zeros ‚Äî "mission-01" produces \`1\`.

**StatusBar.tsx line 47:**
```tsx
const missionNum = String(missionNumber).padStart(2, "0");
```
Re-pads to 2 digits ‚Äî converts \`1\` back to "01".

**MissionMap.tsx line 146:**
```tsx
const num = String(i + 1).padStart(2, "0");
```
Uses array index + 1, zero-padded.

So the pipeline for mission-01 is:
- Briefing: "01" (string replace, naturally zero-padded)
- Mission -> StatusBar: parseInt gives 1 -> padStart gives "01" (round-trip through number)
- MissionMap: index 0 + 1 = 1 -> padStart gives "01"

While the final display is consistent (all show "01"), the Briefing.tsx approach is fragile ‚Äî if a mission ID were ever "mission-1" instead of "mission-01", Briefing would show "1" while StatusBar would show "01".

**Fix:** Extract mission number formatting to a shared utility function:
```tsx
function formatMissionNumber(missionId: string): string {
  const num = parseInt(missionId.replace(/\\D/g, ""), 10) || 1;
  return String(num).padStart(2, "0");
}
```
Use it in Briefing, Mission, StatusBar, and MissionMap for guaranteed consistency.
close_reason: Mission number format standardized. Commit afa3295.

title: Logo screen wraps Text around TypeWriter creating nested Text components
description:
In the Logo screen (src/screens/Logo.tsx), TypeWriter is wrapped inside Text components to apply color:

**Lines 55-61:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  <TypeWriter text={ASCII_LOGO} speed="dramatic" onComplete={advancePhase} />
</Text>
```

**Lines 65-70:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan} bold>
  <TypeWriter text="[ C O D E   A C A D E M Y ]" ... />
</Text>
```

Since TypeWriter internally returns \`<Text>{text.slice(0, charIndex)}</Text>\`, this creates nested \`<Text><Text>...\</Text></Text>\`. In Ink, nested Text components work (inner inherits outer styles), but it means:

1. The outer Text sets color/bold, the inner Text from TypeWriter has no explicit styles ‚Äî this works because of Ink's inheritance, but it's fragile
2. The dimColor Text wrapper on lines 77-83 wraps TypeWriter in \`<Text dimColor>\` ‚Äî dimColor is applied to the outer Text but the inner Text from TypeWriter renders raw text that inherits dimColor correctly

This is not currently broken but is an Ink antipattern. If TypeWriter ever adds its own color prop (as suggested in cca-4ri), the nested Text color could conflict.

Additionally, this pattern is inconsistent ‚Äî Briefing.tsx:70 wraps TypeWriter in Text for color, but Onboarding.tsx and Mission.tsx do NOT wrap TypeWriter in a colored Text.

**Fix:** This is best addressed alongside cca-4ri (TypeWriter color prop). Once TypeWriter accepts a color prop, remove the outer Text wrappers and pass color directly: \`<TypeWriter text={...} color={COLORS.warmWhite} />\`

title: Result feedback spacing differs between QuizStep and CommandStep
description:
After answering, the result display (CONFIRMED/COMPROMISED + explanation) has different spacing in QuizStep vs CommandStep:

**QuizStep result (src/components/QuizStep.tsx lines 146-183):**
- Shows CONFIRMED/COMPROMISED
- On wrong: shows all options with correct one highlighted, wrapped in \`marginTop={1}\`
- Shows explanation in \`<Box marginTop={1}>\` ‚Äî separated from the answers by a blank line

**CommandStep result (src/components/CommandStep.tsx lines 151-172):**
- Shows CONFIRMED/COMPROMISED
- On wrong: shows expected answer on the next line (no margin)
- Shows explanation with no marginTop ‚Äî directly following the expected answer

The QuizStep explanation has a full line of breathing room (\`marginTop={1}\`) between the answer review and the explanation text. The CommandStep crams the explanation right against the expected answer with no gap. This makes the CommandStep feel more cramped.

**Fix:** Add \`marginTop={1}\` before the explanation text in CommandStep's result phase to match QuizStep's spacing. Wrap the CommandStep result in a structure that mirrors QuizStep:

```tsx
{phase === "result" && (
  <Box flexDirection="column">
    {correct ? (
      <Text color={COLORS.green} bold>CONFIRMED</Text>
    ) : (
      // ...COMPROMISED + expected answer
    )}
    <Box marginTop={1}>
      <Text color={COLORS.gray}>{step.explanation}</Text>
    </Box>
  </Box>
)}
```

```tsx
<Box justifyContent="flex-end" marginTop={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
    [ENTER] Return to Mission Map
  </Text>
</Box>
```

This right-aligns the continue prompt. The same pattern in Briefing.tsx line 79 also uses \`justifyContent="flex-end"\`:

```tsx
<Box justifyContent="flex-end" marginTop={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
    [ ENTER TO ACCEPT MISSION ]
  </Text>
</Box>
```

But every other screen places action prompts in natural left-aligned flow:
- Mission.tsx:199 ‚Äî no justifyContent (left-aligned)
- Onboarding.tsx:49 ‚Äî no justifyContent (left-aligned)
- InfiniteMode.tsx:343,417 ‚Äî no justifyContent (left-aligned)
- Credits.tsx:136 ‚Äî justifyContent="center"
- MissionMap.tsx:243 ‚Äî no justifyContent (left-aligned)

The Briefing and Debrief screens right-align their prompts while nothing else does. This creates a visual inconsistency where the user has to look in different places for the "what do I press next" hint.

**Fix:** Change both Briefing and Debrief to use left-aligned or center-aligned prompts to match the convention used everywhere else. Since these screens are centered containers, removing \`justifyContent="flex-end"\` (defaulting to left alignment within the bordered box) would match the majority of screens.

title: Handler overlay header says '[ HANDLER ]' but keybinding label says '[? HANDLER]'
description:
The Handler feature is referenced with inconsistent labels:

**StatusBar.tsx line 73 (visible during missions):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.amber} wrap="truncate">
  [? HANDLER]
</Text>
```
Shows as \`[? HANDLER]\` ‚Äî key + label combined.

**Handler.tsx line 158 (overlay header):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan} bold>
  [ HANDLER ]
</Text>
```
Shows as \`[ HANDLER ]\` ‚Äî no key reference.

**BottomBar.tsx line 12:**
```tsx
handler: "[?] handler",
```
Shows as \`[?] handler\` ‚Äî lowercase.

**HelpOverlay.tsx line 19:**
```tsx
{ key: "?", label: "Ask handler" },
```
Shows as \`[?] Ask handler\` ‚Äî verb phrase.

Four different representations of the same feature:
1. \`[? HANDLER]\` ‚Äî StatusBar
2. \`[ HANDLER ]\` ‚Äî overlay header
3. \`[?] handler\` ‚Äî BottomBar
4. \`[?] Ask handler\` ‚Äî HelpOverlay

**Fix:** Standardize:
- Overlay header: \`[ HANDLER ]\` (fine as-is, matches other headers)
- StatusBar shortcut: \`[?] Handler\` (match BottomBar format with title case)  
- BottomBar: \`[?] Handler\` (title case to match other actions)
- HelpOverlay: \`[?] Handler\` or keep \`Ask handler\` since HelpOverlay uses verb phrases

title: Achievement notification uses gold border but amber name ‚Äî inconsistent emphasis hierarchy
description:
The Achievement component (src/components/Achievement.tsx lines 107-122) uses a mixed gold/amber color scheme:

```tsx
<Box borderStyle="double" borderColor={COLORS.gold} ...>
  <Text color={COLORS.gold} bold>ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED</Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>{name}</Text>
  {description && <Text color={COLORS.gray}>{description}</Text>}
</Box>
```

The hierarchy is:
1. Border: gold
2. Title "ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED": gold
3. Achievement name: amber
4. Description: gray

COLORS.gold (#FFD700) and COLORS.amber (#FFB627) are visually very similar ‚Äî gold is slightly more yellow, amber slightly more orange. Using both in a small 3-line notification creates a subtle color shift that reads as "almost the same but slightly off" rather than a deliberate design choice.

Compare with every other screen where the border and header use the same color (cyan border + cyan header is the standard).

**Fix:** Use a single color for the achievement's prominent elements. Either:
1. All gold: change the name to \`color={COLORS.gold}\` to match the border and title
2. All amber: change the border and title to \`color={COLORS.amber}\` 

Option 1 (all gold) is slightly better since gold is the "special/premium" color already associated with stars, and achievements are premium rewards.

title: loadProgress() called during render body in App component ‚Äî should be memoized or moved to effect
description: In app.tsx line 41, `loadProgress()` is called directly in the render body of the App component. This means it runs on every single re-render, reading from the conf store (or memory fallback) each time. While the operation is synchronous and fast, it's an anti-pattern in React ‚Äî side-effectful reads during render can cause issues with concurrent features and make the component harder to reason about. The result is only used to check `firstRunComplete` for initial routing.
notes: File: src/app.tsx line 41. Fix options: (1) Use useMemo to memoize the initial load, (2) Move to a useState initializer: `const [initialProgress] = useState(() => loadProgress())`, (3) Read only the specific field needed. Option 2 is simplest and most idiomatic.
close_reason: loadProgress moved to useState initializer. Commit 60a2f07.

title: Hardcoded hex color '#1a7a0a' in MissionMap legacy mode
description:
In `src/screens/MissionMap.tsx` line 87, the CRT/legacy mode defines a dim green color as a raw hex literal:

```tsx
const dim = crt ? "#1a7a0a" : COLORS.gray;
```

This is the only hardcoded hex color outside `src/constants.ts`. If the green palette value changes, this dim variant won't track with it.

**Fix:** Add a `dimGreen` (or `crtDim`) entry to the COLORS constant in `src/constants.ts` and reference it here. Alternatively, since this is a one-off CRT easter egg, at minimum extract it to a named constant at the top of the file.

title: Build pre-publish security and smoke test checklist

4. Secret scan: grep dist/ for sk-ant, AKIA, password, secret, token

6. Smoke test with AI: node dist/index.js (play mission 1)

9. Test npx flow: npm pack && npx ./claude-code-academy-*.tgz
10. Verify clean install: npm ci

title: Nine test files duplicate the tick() helper instead of importing from render-ink.ts
description: The tick() helper function (which advances fake timers by a given number of milliseconds) is defined in __tests__/helpers/render-ink.ts and exported, but nine test files redefine it locally with identical implementations: ScreenTransition.test.tsx, Achievement.test.tsx, StatusBar.test.tsx, TypeWriter.test.tsx, mission-flow.integration.test.tsx, Briefing.test.tsx, Debrief.test.tsx, Onboarding.test.tsx, and Mission.test.tsx. This duplication means if the tick() implementation needs to change (e.g. to handle React 19 scheduler quirks differently), it must be updated in 10 places instead of 1.
notes: Files with duplicate tick(): __tests__/components/ScreenTransition.test.tsx:18, Achievement.test.tsx:21, StatusBar.test.tsx:9, TypeWriter.test.tsx:17, __tests__/integration/mission-flow.integration.test.tsx:104, __tests__/screens/Briefing.test.tsx:27, Debrief.test.tsx:47, Onboarding.test.tsx:51, Mission.test.tsx:77. The canonical version is in __tests__/helpers/render-ink.ts. Fix: delete the local definitions and add tick to the import from render-ink.ts in each file.
close_reason: Deduplicated tick() across 9 test files. Commit 2336bd7.

title: Wire progress persistence with conf
description:
Save/load game state using conf package.

Schema (versioned for future migration):
{
  schemaVersion: 1,
  completedMissions: string[],
  starRatings: Record<string, 1|2|3>,
  fxp: number,
  clearanceLevel: 'recruit'|'operative'|'elite',
  achievements: string[],
  quizStats: { correct: number, total: number },
  infiniteModeUnlocked: boolean,
  firstRunComplete: boolean,
}

Requirements:
- Use conf's migrations option for schema versioning
- Wrap all conf calls in try/catch with fallback to in-memory state (handles FUSE/networked filesystem failures on WSL2)
- Auto-save after each step completion (not just mission completion)
- Save partial progress: if user exits mid-mission, save current step index
- On return: 'Resume Mission 03 from step 4? (Y/n)' prompt
- --reset flag with confirmation prompt: 'This will erase all progress. Continue? (y/N)'

Acceptance: Progress persists between sessions, partial save/resume works, --reset works with confirmation
